All eight• Tall me what the f ac ts are. SB. FREELAND; All right. What happened was this. First of all, the entire school lands trust was created at one tisa in 1817 in Mississippi's enabling Act and the Land Sales Act. The Land Sales Act provided that tha 16 setion lands were to be retained throughout the state in trust for the schools. Then, in the sale of the lands in what is called the Chickasaw Cession, the 23 counties that we represent, rather than retain the lands in trust, as was supposed to be dene, they soli them and gave tha money to tha Indians, and not to the state. QUESTION* The United States Government did that, didn't it? MB. Of ccurse, the state never did do this. In 1856, the state comes along. They — veil, first, in 1854 they gave away the reversionary interest in the land. They had the lands leased for 99 years. They said, wa will giva you a fae simpla titia for no additional charge, which they did. This was Delta land. They by statute converted that tc the use of the state, and put it in the general fund, and spant it just like they would any other money in the general fund. That is a conclusion, isn't it? MB. FEEELANDt The fact that we allege is that this is the result of the lenial. y QUESTION» Row do you define minimum adequate education? KB. FREELRNDi Hell, let's say at the minimum it would be something that would raise the pupil above the level of a functional illiterate, and we literally have schools in Mississippi where they are being denied even that, and a lot of them are not — Tunika County, — I keep gcing back tc that. The poorest ccunty ir tie whole United States. The only poorer place in the whole country is the leper colony on Molokai. The point, though, of the matter is that here we are dealing with a federally created beast. He are not just dealing with appropriations of school money for the State of Mississippi. He are dealing with a trust created by the federal government. We are dealing with a trust that the state admits it has violated ever these years and has continued to violate, and it comes down to this, to say that a suit such as we have here on the lasis cf this continuing violation is barred by the Eleventh Amendment, and that is the state's primary argument, is to say that there is no trust at all. There is no trust, there is a continuing duty. Now, of course, this Court has repeatedly and recently upheld the school lands trusts, in Lassen versus Arizona, Arizona — Alamo Land and Cattle Company versus Arizona. QUESTICN* Both those cases came up through the state court system, didn’t they? . MB. FREELAND* Lassen -- Alamo, did not. QUESTION^ Alamo came up through the federal courts? MB. FREELAND.: It came up through the federal courts. There are other cases that the Court, like United States versus Ervine, that did come up through the federal court system, where in fact the United States was seeking to enforce the prevision of the trust ani tha statas wara arguing thara was no trust here at all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, of course, the Eleventh Amendment is no bar to a suit by the United States against the states. MB. FREELANDi Aid we submit, Justica Behnguist, the Eleventh Amendment is no bar to prospective injunctive relief, which tells these trustees to cease violating the terms of this trust in the future, tc do your duty in the future, and that is what we are asking here. Df course, we ion*t — the entire thrust cf cur case is net just the trust. Assuming arguenio there is no trust, we still say that the way these schools are being financed has no rational basis whatsoever. QUESTION! This is an equal protection case? MR. FREELAND* Yes, it certainly is. What is the basis for it? Well, an accident of land value is a basis, the state says. There is no land to put a value on in North Mississippi. They are trying to make a Rodriguez argument. Se want tc have local administration of local schools. That is the basis for it. There is no school lands trust to administer in Northern Mississippi. The state either gave it away or took it. Contrast this case with Rodriguez. For example, there you have a facially neutral situation. You do have a difference in land values. It is just one section of San Antonie had a higher value of land than another. Here we have a discriminatory act the way this money is divided up and no basis for it. The relief that we seek is simple enough, an adjudication that the trust is valid, that it is governed by federal law, that it is violated annually by the respondent state derogation of the equal protection clause as well as the contracts clause of the Constitution. We seek, prospective injunctive relief to stop these trustees from doing what they have been doing with these kids in the future, to give them a chance, and see that their schools receive the support for which the trust was created in the first place, and which is new being denied. Whit we siy about this trust is certainly consistent with the decisions of this Court dealing with school lands trusts generally. For example, we are asking the Court to apply certain common law principles tc this situation just as the Court did in Oieida II. There you hid a trust created ty the Hcn-Interccurse Act of 1793, and the Court said, well, we don’t have any law tc look tc here, there was no regulatory scheme for enforcing the trust, sc we are going to apply common law principles. These same principles apply here. Be are asking this Court to declare that this is in fact the trust, something that the court has dene each and every time it has ever had an opportunity tc rule on the question, and that the law governing the trust, because it is created by federal law, is, of course, federal law, and the trust is enforceable. In ether words, the short answer tc what we wart is fer the trustees to honor their obligations in the future. Tc argue, as the state does, that the Eleventh Amendment bars any such remedy or the trustee’s diminution of the trust corpus is to say that there is no trust at all, contrary tc the statutes which created it, that there is no enforceable continuing duty, nothing that can be enforced ir federal court, that any claim under federal law would have to be left to the tender mercies of the state court. The Fifth Circuit’s holding is nothing less than that this trust is simply not there because they said there is no continuing duty. Granted, says the Fifth Circuit, there was a valid compact created 130 years ago. What we are looking at now is some weird creature that has to do with Mississippi trust law, and they flatly refuse to enforce the continuing duty, and really that is what this lawsuit is about. If this is a trust, and we certainly contend that it is, the it follows as the night the day that the trustees of this trust, and these are the people that we are suing, are obliged to honor it, and as part of that they are obliged tc pay or see that the trust income is prcpe-rly divided or paid in the future from some source, and they can be made to do that because they are acting in derogation of the equal protection clause. Ke submit that they could be made to do that by this Court, and that indeed they should be made tc dc that by this Court. Are there any other questions? Kay I reserve the rest of my time? turns.text: From the 16th Section lanis? MB. ARNOLD* Yes, sir. I mean, we are speaking not per student or anything. We are speaking total amount cf money that they get at all there. QUESTIOMi I understand. MB. ARNOLD* And a distinction that should be made, I believe — QUESTION* Shat would be the -- is that ?75 per student, is that the average per student in the school districts that get something, or dees that average out through all of the school districts? MR. ARNOLD* I think that is the average for all school districts. That is taking, as I understand it — QUESTION* Including the ones who don't get anything? MB. ARNOLD* Yes, Your Honor, and cf course that is inflated. Some school districts have vast mineral wealth under them, under their 16th Section lands, and they ha*a leased that. Of course, prices have been falling lately. I think these figures may change around based on some changes in the economic pattern here in the last few months or years for that matter• Another point I world like tc make is the Chickasaw Cession counties are gettin? an appropriation from the state, albeit it only £66,000, 66 percent of the corpus on the trust. They are getting something that net another county or schccl district ir. the state gets. They are getting, as we si y, a land — something they are getting that no one else is getting there from the state. So I think that when wa Look at it from the standpoint that the state allows the local school districts to manage their own 16th Section lands subject to state oversight to make sure there is no donation of the land or disposition of the land that is net permitted under stata law under tha constitution, that there is no unequal treatment here. This Court has also said that the equal protection clause is not applicable, I believe, and geographical differentials there. It is between persons, and this is a geographical differential simply because the Initial action of not reserving the 16th Section lands precipitated this whole lawsuit. That is what started it. The 16th Section lands were not reserved. Finally, some five or six years, seven years later, the state vas allowed to select some lanis, the Gcverncr cf the State, and that is the only thing that any named official in this complaint has ever lone in regard to the Chickasaw Cession lands. The federal government allowed the Governor to go out and select these lands in the Mississippi Delta which were virtually unsettled at the time, as I said, swamp and overflow land • The Attorney General has never done anything in regard to these lands, nor has he been authorized to. There has been no duties imposed upon the Attorney General cf the state. The mere fact that he may be a member of the b-oarl of education, or was at one time until the law was changed, just simply does not subject him to being enjoined in this thing. There is nothing he can do. He does not administer the trust. He can’t change it around. He can’t invest it in scrre ether type of investment. He can’t ciange it around or change the interest paid. The same is true of the Secretary of State. He does not administer the trust. Ha no longer sits on the beard cf education. He is a member cf the State Lieu Land Commission, and under our law, which is still in effect, and under the constitution lieu lands can be disposed of tc this day, right new. I believe there are something like 37 acres left that nobody really wants, but you can dispose of lieu lands under state law at this time. So, it is our contention — and also the Superintendent cf Education at cne time was a member cf the board of education. Ha is not any more. Ha was appointed by the board of education. The Assistant Secretary of State, that person is basically someone there who keeps up with the stata lands, where is the state land, has it been leased, is it coming up fer renewal, and this sort of thing. These offices, and* that is what is sued here, they are only sued in their official capacity, have newer acquired or disposed of tha Chickasaw lieu lands cr handled any funds frem there. In order tc accomplish what the petitioners seak i e ra, tha State of Mississippi, the State of Mississippi would have tc grant the relief. If there is an injunction issued in this case, as petitioners seek, it would have to be out cf the corpus cf the State cf Mississippi tc pay this money in the future. ( That is where it would come from, and we submit that this would be prescribed by the Eleventh Amendment on the darisions of this Court that start back with Hans v. Icuisiana and come forward all the way through Green varsus Mansour, I believe, decided this term by the Court. Court at this time is that tha — if tha Court were to make the order that petitioners seek, we believe it would litarally opan the proverbial Pandora’s Box. Every school district in the southern district of the State of Mississippi would then sue the state, saying, well, another school district has got more money or more land cr receives more income from there because of geographical variances than we do, so we will sue, and we will get our money back, or get some more money tack, although we have sold our lands some years age. QUESTION^. Mr. Arnold, what — to the extent that the plaintiff said in the complaint Mississippi is depriving the children of the plaintiff class of a minimally adequate education, and that is prohibited by tha egual protection clause, now, just to that extent, is that barred in your view also by the Eleventh Amendment, that kind of a claim? MR. ARSOLDi lour Honor, I believe it would be. As I read Filer v. Doe and San Antcnic there, what this Court has said in the past is that it is the absolute deprivation cf an education that is tarred by the QUESTION* Well, assuming that what the Court said was that you will apply a higher level cf scrutiny if a chili is denial a minimally adequate public school education, and that is alleged in the complaint, dc ycu think the Eleventh Amendment absolutely bars that complaint in federal court? ME. ARNOLD* If the state is the real true party in interest, Your Honor, to require the state to expend funds, perhaps it would. I think it wculd depend on the facts as they have evolved by the Court, but in the facts that we have alleged here — QUESTION* Well, I guess the facts weren’t developed, were they? ME. ARNOLD* Eight, but as the facts are alleged here, I believe the Eleventh Amendment would proscribe that, would be a jurisdictional bar as far as the State of Mississipp?i, and it is the real party in interest in this case, we submit. The ctber defendants are nominal defendants only for the purpose of attempting to evade the Eleventh Amendment jurisdictional bar which would apply to the state. Sc, I think it would in this case. QUESTION* If ycu are right, why wouldn’t the San Antonio case have gone off on the Eleventh Amendment grounds rather than the substantive eQual protection decision? MR. ARNOLDi I am not sure, Your Honor. I don't know if they raised that or not in the San Antonio case. I don't recall it ia seeing if they raised the Eleventh Amendment as a jurisdicticnal bar ir that case there. But be that as it nay, assuming arguendo that the Eleventh Amendment is net a jurisdictional bar in this case, there just simply has not been a cause of action stated that can survive a motion to dismiss under 12(b)(6). A boilerplate allegation, just boilerplate, deprived of a minimally adeguate education, it is just net there. That is just not something I think this Court or any federal court could address. The Court would be continually looking over this. QUESTION* The reason it was difficult to address it, of course. Is that the record that we got just didn't really reflect what ether funding the state gives to peoples. That is the handicap. MR. ARNOLD* Perhaps in the record not, Ycur Honor, but in our brief, I believe, on Page 6 we referred tc the minimum program for education that the State of Mississippi provides and what these funds go to, and how it goes to each school district based upon the teacher units. Perhaps I did net make this clear as I should have to the Court. But other than that, that fund going to these school districts throughout the state, the only ether appropriation specifically out of the state treasury to education is the money tc the Chickasaw Cession. Ir that regard, I believe the figures will shew, the mest recent figures show in the State of Mississippi approximately 62 or 63 percent, in that range, cf the total state budget is directed to educational matters throughout the state and the public education in the state. I think this year it probably increased. There have been some increases as far as the amount the state would pay in teachers' salaries. But we would submit to tha Court that the State cf Mississippi is the real true party in interest in this case. They are seeking what they call an injunction, but it would require the state to expand its funds cut cf the state treasury. We also submit that there is no equal protection claim based upon the allegations made in the complaint here, because to the contrary of net receiving everything that the state appropriates to every school district, the districts in the northern section cf the state receive mere as far as appropriation from the State of Mississippi itself there. And the fact that one school district may be able tc raise mere money because the land is valuable cr it has minerals uniar it is not an equal protection question that should be addressed by this Court or any ether loser federal court. Thank you. CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER* Hr. Freeland. MR. FREELAND* Mr. Chief Justice, counsel says that there are school districts in the State of Mississipppi that get no 16th Section land money. That may be. I would like, though, to refer the Court, if I may, to Joint Appendix 45, where the Secretary of State points cut precisely hew much moneys are received freir the trust by the various counties in the State of Mississippi . QUESTION* Counties or school districts? MR. FREELAND* Counties and school districts. I aa using them synonymous, Justice White, because that is the way the money is divided up. QUESTION* Are the leases made by the counties? MR. FREELAND* Under the supervisicn of the Secretary of State. QUESTION* I knew, but the ccunties don't make — the lease rantaL is not paid to the county, is it? MB. FREELAND* No, it is paid to the county school board as it now stands. QUESTION* It is paid to school districts, isn't it? MR. FREELANDu School districts. Yes, sir. That's correct. That's correct. QUESTION* Well, school districts are not necessarily cotaruiaus with counties, are they? I mean, may there not be more than one school district in a ccunty ? MR. FREELAND* Absolutely, and that is the point also as to Meridian, fcr example. What happened with that 16th Section laad, it was soli, and the money is held in trust, and they get the income frcir the trust. So, to ay that they are not getting any income from 16th Section lands of the trust begs the question. They still are, and our complaint is that we are not, and that is what this lawsuit is about. It is about the trust. It is net about hew the State cf Mississippi funis its schools around the state. They ic that on more or less an equal basis, and that is another basis fcr our complaint, however. How is it that they can give everybody the same amount cf state money, and yet acting as trustees of the trust give us not one nickel of the trust income and say that they ace treating us on any sort of rational, reasonable basis? And then they point at Rodriguez ani say, well, tiers wasn’t any ii scrimina tion in Rodriguez. Cf course there wasn't. The case was tried, and that is what the trial court found, that the scheme in Rodriguez was facially neutral, but of course we allege here that we are dealing with rank discrimination, ani that is what the pleadings have to say. The Eleventh Amendment, we contend that this claim concerns only continuing violations of feieral statutory and constitutional law, and it falls therefore within the rules permitting suits alleging conduct contrary to the supreme authority of the United States, as this Court mentioned in Fenhurst, or it said in Green versus Mansour, a recent opinion, that such prospective relief as we are locking for vindicates the authority cf the United States, aid that is all we ask . We ask that this trust be enforced. We don't ask the Court to interfere with the way schools are funded in Mississippi. QUESTION* What if we disagree with you that there is any trust at all? Then do you still claim that there is an ecual protection violation? QUESTION; Even if there is no trust. HE. FREELAND* If there is no trust. Irecisely . QUESTION* And even if the Chickasaw counties cr schccl districts have no claim whatsoever to any moneys from 16th Section lands or from lieu lands.

turns.name: FREELAND
turns.text: . Then what they are doing is totally irrational, because they are giving $75 cr $74 a pupil in 59 counties, and at the same time they are giving 63 cents a pupil in the rest of the — QUESTION; I would think you would rather have us find there was no trust. ME. FREELAND; I would rather have you find that there is a trust. Your Honor, because just as the Court has done in — QUESTION* Aren't you arguing against yourself really ? MR. FREELAND* I am arguing that I have get a two-way street here. Not only do we have the trust, which this Ccurt has repeatedly upheld in Ardres, Lassen, Alamo Land and Cattle, more cases than I can name, but also that the scheme that the state has set up is so totally irrational that we also have an equal protection claim waich we press. CHIEF JUSTICE BURGERi Thank, you, gentlemen. The case Is submitted.
